coredata.dc:title: Exploring the non-traditional fluorescence emission of non-conjugated polymers dots for sensing pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Fernandes, Rafaella F.
Atvars, Teresa D.Z.
Temperini, Marcia L.A.
coredata.dc:description: The chemical sensing of pesticides is a high priority for a broad range of areas, including public health and environmental sustainability, since the pesticides have expanded dramatically in various agrarian operations. In this sense, the optical properties of non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) can be a decisive advantage for sensing since the high sensitivity, fast response, and excellent photostability are very convenient properties. Here, we present the unique emission of NCPDs based on- β-glucan polymer, the major polysaccharide from the Usnea lichen biomass, which was explored for the sensing of the methyl parathion and dicloran pesticides due to the inner filter effect mechanism quenching. The sensing performance of the low-cost and eco-friendly NCPDs from renewable resources can shed light on the recent advances and applications of carbon-based nanomaterials involving non-traditional fluorescent emission systems, especially to monitor biologically and environmentally important analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensing
Inner filter effect
Pesticides
Non-conjugated polymer dots
β-Glucan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: β-glucan chemical extraction and purification method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Advanced Ceramics

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Chemistry Institute of São Paulo University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are indebted to FAPESP (Grant Nos. 2018/25422-9 and 2016/21070-5) for financial support. In addition, the authors would like to acknowledge the “Laboratório de Sólidos Lamelares” (Prof. Dr. Vera R. L. Constantino and Dr. Ricardo A. A. de Couto, Chemistry Institute of São Paulo University) for supporting the experiments, the Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Advanced Ceramics (Prof. Valmor R. Mastelaro, São Carlos Institute of Physics, University of São Paulo) for the XPS facilities and, to prof. Dr. Hermi F. Brito and Dr. Israel F. da Costa for the phosphorescence measurements. This research used facilities of the Brazilian Nanotechnology National Laboratory (LNNano). The Laboratório de Microscopia Eletrônica (LME) staff is acknowledged for their assistance during the experiments (TEM-FT - 28027 and 28164).

The authors are indebted to FAPESP (Grant Nos. 2018/25422-9 and 2016/21070-5) for financial support. In addition, the authors would like to acknowledge the “Laboratório de Sólidos Lamelares” (Prof. Dr. Vera R. L. Constantino and Dr. Ricardo A. A. de Couto, Chemistry Institute of São Paulo University) for supporting the experiments, the Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Advanced Ceramics (Prof. Valmor R. Mastelaro, São Carlos Institute of Physics, University of São Paulo) for the XPS facilities and, to prof. Dr. Hermi F. Brito and Dr. Israel F. da Costa for the phosphorescence measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) STEM and (B) SEM images of the NCPDs.

Panel 1: FT-Raman (red) and IR (black) spectra of the NCPDs and Panel 2: High-resolution XPS spectra of A) C 1 s, B) O 1 s, and C) N 1 s regions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of the NCPDs showing the maxima emission at 430 nm (λexc = 350 nm), (B) pH dependence of the NCPD fluorescence emission at λexc of 350 nm, repeatedly switching the pH from alkaline to acidic conditions (initial pH 8.39), and (C) Measurements of instrument response function IRF and lifetime of NCPDs in water solution at room temperature (λexc = 335 nm, λem = 430 nm). All the measurements were made using a 1 mg/mL solution of the NCPDs dispersed in water.
Time-resolved emission spectra of the NCPDs in powder (λexc 350 nm): (A) with room temperature with delays of td = 0.05 and 1 ms and (B) Phosphorescence emission at 77 K with different delayed times.

(A) Absorption UV–Vis spectra of DCR (350 nm), and p-NP (398 nm), and (B) Excitation spectrum (350 nm) which provides the maxima emission fluorescence spectrum (430 nm) of the NCPDs. Inset: Images of NCPDs water solution under visible and UV lights. All the spectra were recorded using a 1 mg/mL solution of the NCPDs dispersed in water.

The fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs with concentrations increasing of the (A) DCR and (B) p-NP, showing the spectral changes due to the addition of pesticides leading to a 7%, 30%, and 84% intensities decrease in the observed fluorescence emission. The graphics show the plot of the intensities I0/I vs. the concentration of the (C) DCR and (D) p-NP, where I0 is the emission intensity in the absence of the pesticide and I the intensity in the pesticide addition.
The principle of IFE-based fluorescence quenching of NCPDs by the pesticides in an aqueous medium.

head.ce:title: Exploring the non-traditional fluorescence emission of non-conjugated polymers dots for sensing pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fundamental Chemistry Department, Institute of Chemistry, University of São Paulo, Av. Prof. Lineu Prestes 748, São Paulo 05508-000, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fundamental Chemistry Department, Institute of Chemistry, University of São Paulo, Av. Prof. Lineu Prestes 748, São Paulo, 05508-000, Brazil.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Universidade Estadual de Campinas, Campinas, São Paulo, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Universidade Estadual de Campinas, Campinas, São Paulo, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The chemical sensing of pesticides is a high priority for a broad range of areas, including public health and environmental sustainability, since the pesticides have expanded dramatically in various agrarian operations. In this sense, the optical properties of non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) can be a decisive advantage for sensing since the high sensitivity, fast response, and excellent photostability are very convenient properties. Here, we present the unique emission of NCPDs based on- β-glucan polymer, the major polysaccharide from the Usnea lichen biomass, which was explored for the sensing of the methyl parathion and dicloran pesticides due to the inner filter effect mechanism quenching. The sensing performance of the low-cost and eco-friendly NCPDs from renewable resources can shed light on the recent advances and applications of carbon-based nanomaterials involving non-traditional fluorescent emission systems, especially to monitor biologically and environmentally important analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: Significant progress has recently been made in applying nanomaterials to develop the chemical sensing of pesticides [1–3]. In terms of fluorescence-based detection of pesticides, different scaffolds have been employed dealing with supramolecular platforms, including fluorescent polymers [4], macrocycles [5], nanomaterials [6], and metal-organic frameworks [7].

Herewith, our study presents the NCPDs as a new class of sensing platforms owing to their notable sensitivity, rapid signal response time toward analytes, and ease of manipulation, aiming to cover the massive demand for fluorescent-based development probes.

NCPDs are prepared from non-conjugated polymers by dehydration, condensation, carbonization, or assembly routes, promising as novel fluorescent materials due to the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [8]. The rigidity of the polymer network, achieved by the crosslinking and aggregation of chemical groups highly involved in H-bonds or electrostatic interactions, enhanced the fluorescence emission of the sub-fluorophores' groups, such as C=O/C-O and amino-based groups by the CEE effect [9,10].

The application of NCPDs as sensing can be a great advantage since they not only represent an “eco-friendly” matrix that offers better structural features than metallic nanomaterials but also act as functional materials for detection by fluorescence mechanisms and enable high stability and sensitivity in sensors.

Based on fluorescence response to analytes, several mechanisms have been proposed to construct fluorescence sensing probes, namely fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced-charge transfer (PCT), photoinduced-electron transfer (PET), aggregation-induced emission quenching (AIEQ), and inner filter effect (IFE) [11].

The inner filter effect mechanism is a radiative energy conversion process relying on the absorption of the excitation and/or emission energy of the fluorophores by absorbers in the detection system [12]. Two consequences may arise from IFE: a decrease of the donor fluorescence intensity in the region of spectral overlap and distortion of the fluorescence spectrum if the acceptor absorbs the donor emission.

The IFE refers to a mechanism where, such as FRET, there is a significant spectral overlap between the absorption of the acceptor molecule and the excitation/emission of the fluorophores [12]. However, a critical difference between IFE and FRET is that there is no requirement for the proximity of the donors and acceptors for IFE. This point, in particular, can be considered a prominent advantage for the method since IFE-based sensors do not require covalent linking between a receptor and a fluorophore or surface modification, providing simplicity and flexibility in probe fabrication. Also, the IFE has shown as an effective and efficient strategy tool for converting the analytical absorption signals into fluorescence intensity, which has been proven to commendably enhance the sensitivity and selectivity compared to other fluorescence quenching mechanisms [13].

In general, changes in the fluorescence signal upon introduction of pesticides are most often “turn-off” mechanisms, resulting in the decrease of the observed fluorescence emission and, in this case, the IFE effect has achieved significant success in facilitating pesticide detection, primarily due to the high absorbance of the pesticide in the UV spectral region that provides an internal light filter that reaches the target luminophores [14].

The present work describes a fluorescence-based IFE mechanism using NCPDs via green synthesis to sense dicloran and methyl parathion pesticides. Thus, besides the nanomaterial preparation from a unique biopolymer that unifies crucial issues such as green chemistry and low-cost renewable sources, we present the application of NCPDs as the pesticide detection topic.

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first mention of the NCPDs application from bio-based polymer to sensing pesticides. In contrast, NCPDs from other polymer sources have been applied to detect Fe+3 [15] and nitro-explosive Picric Acid [16].

All the techniques used for the chemical characterization (UV–vis, Raman, IR, Fluorescence, Phosphorescence, and X-ray spectroscopies and Scanning Transmission and Scanning Electron Microscopies) are concisely described in the Supplementary Material section.

The methyl parathion and dicloran pesticides were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Ltd. Potassium hydroxide and sulfuric acid from Merck, ethanol from LabShynth (Brazil), quinine sulfate and trichloroacetic acid were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Ltd., and PBS buffer was purchased from ThermoFischer Scientific.

ce:section.ce:section-title: β-glucan chemical extraction and purification method
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical extraction method to obtain the polymer fraction and the unequivocal chemical characterization were reported in our previous work [17]. Briefly, a dried sample of the Usnea lichen was submitted to a hot aqueous 0.5% KOH extraction for 24 h. The trichloroacetic acid (10% m/v) was added to the alkaline solution for deproteinization. The main protein content was removed by centrifugation (5000 rpm for 10 min). Then, the solution was precipitated with 3 vol. of cold ethanol to give fractions rich in polysaccharides. The fraction was re-suspended in ultra-pure water, frozen, and slowly thawed at room temperature. The insoluble β-glucan polysaccharide of interest was found in the precipitate fraction.

First, 100 mg of the polymer fraction was heated from room temperature to 80 °C using a reflux setup and kept at the set temperature for 40 min until the complete water solubilization. Next, the solution was freeze-dried and heated to 190 °C for 2 h. Finally, the residues were dispersed in 3 mL of ultrapure water and separated in a 0.22 μm filter for the following freeze-drying process. Furthermore, the synthesis mentioned above of NCPDs is an improved methodology already reported [18]. Yield: 15 mg.

The quantum yield measurement was performed with quinine sulfate in 0.10 N H2SO4 (quantum yield 0.54 at 350 nm [19]) as the standard. The quantum yield was calculated from Eq. 1, where Φx is the quantum yield, Grad is the slope from the integrated intensity vs. absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and the indices x and st denote the sample and the standard, respectively (Fig. S1). Φx = 1.83%
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ st
Grad x
Grad st
η x 2
η st 2

The alkaline hydrolysis of methyl parathion proceeded successfully after 1 mg of the pesticide was solubilized in PBS buffer (1 mL, pH 12.0). The solution was kept at 70 °C for 30 min to ensure the complete response for the p-nitrophenol formation (Fig. S2), which can be noticed due to the color of the mixture changing to bright yellow.

Fluorescence measurements were executed by mixing 50 μL of different concentrations of the pesticide solution with NCPDs (1 mg/ mL, in PBS pH 12.0). The fluorescence intensity of the mixture at 350 nm was collected for quantitative analysis of methyl parathion. The fluorescence measurements were performed in triplicate (Fig. S3A).

The direct fluorescence measurements of dicloran were carried-out after 50 μL of different concentrations with the pesticide solution were mixed with NCPDs water solution (1 mg/ mL). The fluorescence quenching of NCPDs was monitored at 350 nm. The fluorescence measurements were performed in triplicate (Fig. S3B).

Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM) images indicate the formation of the nanoaggregates (Fig. 1A), where the very polydisperse structures of NCPDs can be noticed. In addition, aggregated NCPDs with sizes larger than 50 nm were observed in this study.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was also used for morphology determination characterization of nanomaterial, for which mostly spherical nanoparticles of NCPDs were obtained. The SEM and STEM images of NCPDs provided in supporting information indicated the coalescence of small particles (Fig. S4).

Vibrational spectroscopy is widely applied to structural investigations of polymers [20–22], and for this, Fig. 2 (Panel 1) exhibits the Raman and IR spectra of NCPDs. Unambiguously, both IR and FT-Raman spectra provide the prominent bands of the NCPDs based on β-glucan biopolymer. One fingerprint for polysaccharides is the C1H equatorial bending vibration related to the β–anomer at 897 cm−1, a β-glucan marker [23]. In general, the main FT-Raman bands observed at 1462 cm−1 can be attributed to the δ(CH2) vibrational mode of the CH2OH group, 1373 cm−1 assigned to the [δ(COH) + δ(CH)] mode, and 1093 cm−1 to [ν(C–O–C)] of the glycosidic bonds [25]. The band is worth mentioning at 1615 cm−1, indicating the COO− group, which is not usually notorious in the β-glucan polymer.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out to shed light on the chemical bond composition (Fig. 2, panel 2). The broad scan spectra show that the nanomaterial is abundant in oxygen and carbon, whereas residual nitrogen can be noticed. In Fig. 2A, the region related to C 1 s is composed of four distinct chemical environments for carbon atoms, in which the most significant contribution is emerging in the form of C-C/ CH (284.8 eV), CO (286.3 eV), O-C-O (287.7 eV), and O=C-O (288.9 eV) species [26]. Furthermore, the O 1 s region in Fig. 2B shows a dominant contribution of C-O-H and C-O-C (532.9 eV), while the remaining peaks can be ascribed to O=C-O (532.2 eV) and O=C-O (534. eV) species [24]. High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s in Fig. 2C reveal the central peak at 400.1 eV corresponding to the amide (N-C=O) groups. Despite the less nitrogen contribution in the XPS spectrum (∼1%), this unique peak and the Raman band at 1615 cm−1 can suggest the presence of traces of amide bonds [25] remaining from the chemical extraction of β-glucan. We hypothesized that the extraction of the β-glucan with some traces of nitrogen could be caused by the already existing protein-polysaccharide linkages in the native lichen matrix, whose unbroken release is facilitated by the alkaline-water method and high pH of the extraction [26].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the NCPDs are shown in Fig. 3A. Along with the excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 450 nm, the PL emission peaks of the NCPDs are red-shift from 422 to 523 nm with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 350 nm and 430 nm, respectively. It is readily seen that the wavelength-dependent excitation and the emission peaks shift to longer wavelengths with the increase of excitation wavelength, which is a typical extensively reported PL emission behavior of carbon dots [27–29]. Quantum yield was determined as Φx = 1.83% in water solution.

The influence of pH on the NCPDs PL spectra was investigated in alkaline/acid solutions (Fig. 3B). Starting with a pH of 8.39, the solution was acidified to pH < 2 and subsequently was tuned to alkaline conditions with pH > 12. The PL of three cycles of acidification and basic conditions were recorded. Initially, the PL intensity of NCPDs diminishes in acidic and alkaline conditions compared to the initial. After this first cycle, there is a stabilization of the emission intensities in acidic conditions as well in base conditions (cycles 2 and 3), these later intensities being lower than in an acidic medium. The pH effect correlates well with the protonation and deprotonation of the different functional groups. These findings are widely reported in carbon dots functionalized with the -OH, COOH, and NH2 chemical groups [30].

Meanwhile, although identified as a reversible effect, the dramatically decreasing fluorescence response intensity in alkaline conditions (Fig. 3B) suggests that the detecting assay based on IFE of methyl parathion should also dilute the NCPDs sample in PBS (pH 12.0).

The luminescence decay curve of the NCPDs water solutions was recorded by monitoring the emission maxima at 430 nm (Fig. 3C) by exciting the sample at 335 nm. Their luminescence showed a multiexponential decay feature, which is consistent with the sample polymorphism. The excellent fit was assumed when χ2 = 1.185, along with a visual analysis of the residuals from the fitted data (Fig. S5).

The polysaccharide chains may aggregate to different degrees in NCPDs, leading to other excited states with minor energy differences. Meanwhile, the multiple decay components can indicate the simultaneous contribution from an ensemble of emissive species within a heterogeneous population.

Thus, to provide a more detailed understanding of the non-conjugated polymer dots herein described, the phosphorescence emission spectra at room temperature and 77 K are shown in Fig. 4 with emission bands at 395 nm and 420 nm at 77 K, respectively. Meanwhile, the time-resolved spectra with different delays (td = 0.05 and 1 ms) indicated the simultaneous contribution from the phosphorescence emission at 520 nm.

The higher relative intensity of the phosphorescence spectra at 77 K (Fig. 4B) can be explained by both the hindrance of some molecular vibrations in the frozen system becoming the phosphorescence band sharper and blue-shifted and by the lower quenching rate by molecular oxygen.

The chemical structures of NCPDs probably benefit the formation of intramolecular or intermolecular H-bonds, resulting in a conformation rigidification that can usually be utilized to stabilize the triplet state. In this sense, the H-bonds can play a vital role in achieving RTP emission under atmospheric conditions, as described in the literature [31].

Scheme 1 shows the principle of IFE-based fluorescence sensing for MP: NCPDs particles are preferentially excited using at 350 nm and their emissions at 430 nm (Fig. 5B) are partially absorbed by the pesticide molecules. In this regard, the IFE has achieved significant success in improving pesticide detection, primarily due to the high absorbance of the pesticides in the UV spectral region [14]. Similar results can be achieved for NCPDs (energy donor) in the presence of dicloran fungicide (energy acceptor) due to the significant spectral overlap between the acceptor absorption and donor excitation spectral overlapped (Fig. 5).

The methyl parathion is an organophosphate insecticide that can be hydrolyzed under strongly alkaline conditions [32], promoting p-nitrophenol (absorption band at 398 nm, Fig. 5). Subsequently, the indirect IFE mechanism occurred between p-nitrophenol and NCPDs, leading to significant fluorescence quenching of NCPDs.

The efficient IEF is higher when the absorption band of the quenchers possesses is strongly overlapped with the fluorophore excitation or emission bands. Due to this condition, we expect a better sensitive response for the dicloran due to the more robust spectral overlap.

Fluorescence intensities of NCPDs changes were recorded after adding MP and DCR to provide a range of final concentrations from 0.05 to 30 ppm. As shown in Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs decreased gradually with the increase in pesticide concentrations. The corresponding linear relationship between fluorescent responses to different concentrations can be found in Fig. S6.

Moreover, an evident redshift of 10 nm can be detected as the pesticides were progressively added. We hypothesized that the IFE induced the concentration dependence redshift as characteristic of the aggregated formation.

For comparison of the IFE performances of both pesticides, we analyzed which concentrations are producing the same percentage decrease in the fluorescence intensities using data in Fig. 6. It is worth noting that for creating an intensity decrease of 7%, the concentration of the DCR is 1.18 × 10−6 M, which is higher than for p-NP (0.35 × 10−6 M). On the other hand, this trend reverses for a higher decrease in intensities. For example, the 83% intensity reduction required a concentration variation of 88.80 × 10−6 M of DCR, which is lower than 182 × 10−6 M of p-NP.

These results can be explained by the better spectral overlap of NCPDs and DCR than between p-NP. This also explained the lower detection limit for p-NP. The strategy adopted using the NCPDs provided in this work, without heating procedure or any addition of other reagents or enzymes, can be a potential alternative for sensing assays owing to the competitive LOD calculated of 3.16 × 10−6 (MP) and 4.78 × 10−7 M (DCR) (Fig. S6). These differences in the concentration dependence can be explained by the differences in the quenching process regimes, as discussed later.

The graphics in Fig. 6 show that the emission intensity dependences of NCPDs on the concentration of both pesticides. Plots of I0/I vs. concentration of pesticides follows an exponential function for the DCR, consistent with a typical internal filter effect, similar to a Lambert-Beer law. Nevertheless, despite the noticeable initial reduction of the p-NP addition, compatible with the internal filter, the plot curve has a profile changing from a critical p-NP concentration indicating that other types of mechanisms become prevalent, following an approximately linear law more similar to a Stern-Volmer quenching process [33]. These differences in the quenching processes can explain the data in Fig. 6 for the dependence of the intensities on the analyte concentrations. Although these differences, the NCPDs sensor performance is still suitable for these pesticides' detection.

Several supramolecular scaffolds, such as polymers, macrocycles, nanomaterials, and metal-organic frameworks, have been reported using fluorescent polymers for pesticide detection, relying on the ability of pesticides to act as fluorescence quenchers. In this sense, it is worthwhile to mention the use of NCPDs from β-glucan in preparation without the significant impact of chemicals on the environment is a critical point of this work that could make it a great candidate for extensive applications.

Herein, fluorescence quenching of NCPDs occurs through predominately by an inner filter effect due to the strong absorbance of the pesticides, both in the same spectral region. Also, this effect is evidenced by the minimal changes in the emission profile of the donor spectrum. Due to the dependence of the emission intensity on the pesticide concentration, the IFE mechanism proved helpful for pesticide detection using NCPDs.

In this sense, the NCPDs here as-prepared can detect the methyl parathion and dicloran pesticides in a LOD of 3.16 × 10−6 M and 4.78 × 10−7 M, respectively. These results suggested that biopolymers are promising materials for optical applications, providing an alternative to synthetic luminophores in a cost-competitive chemical way.

Finally, the presented strategy of using NCPDs based-biopolymer would open a new perspective for facile and rapid detection of two pesticides by fluorescence emission in a simple methodology avoiding complex modification of the fluorophore structure or chemical attaching between receptor and fluorophore.

The manuscript was written with the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript. R.F.F. conceived the main idea and performed the experiments, which were supervised by. T.D.Z.A. and M.L.A.T.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are indebted to FAPESP (Grant Nos. 2018/25422-9 and 2016/21070-5) for financial support. In addition, the authors would like to acknowledge the “Laboratório de Sólidos Lamelares” (Prof. Dr. Vera R. L. Constantino and Dr. Ricardo A. A. de Couto, Chemistry Institute of São Paulo University) for supporting the experiments, the Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Advanced Ceramics (Prof. Valmor R. Mastelaro, São Carlos Institute of Physics, University of São Paulo) for the XPS facilities and, to prof. Dr. Hermi F. Brito and Dr. Israel F. da Costa for the phosphorescence measurements.

This research used facilities of the Brazilian Nanotechnology National Laboratory (LNNano). The Laboratório de Microscopia Eletrônica (LME) staff is acknowledged for their assistance during the experiments (TEM-FT - 28027 and 28164).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.reactfunctpolym.2022.105483.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials for sensing and destroying pesticides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Aragay, F. Pino, A. Merkoçi, Nanomaterials for Sensing and Destroying Pesticides, Chem. Rev. 112 (2012) 5317–5338. doi: 10.1021/cr300020c.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advancements in the detection of organophosphate pesticides: a review

Iqbal, Occurrence, toxic effects, and mitigation of pesticides as emerging environmental pollutants using robust nanomaterials – A review, Chemosphere. 293 (2022) 133538. doi: 10.1016/j.chemosphere.2022.133538.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecularly imprinted polymers’ application in pesticide residue detection

Sun, Water-stable lanthanide–organic macrocycles from a 1,2,4-triazole-based chelate for enantiomeric excess detection and pesticide sensing, Dalt. Trans. 50 (2021) 5759–5764. doi: 10.1039/D1DT00726B.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in nanomaterials-based electrochemical (bio)sensors for pesticides detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly luminescent entangled metal–organic framework based on pyridine-substituted tetraphenylethene for efficient pesticide detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-L. Tao, B. Chen, X.-G. Liu, L.-J. Zhou, X.-L. Zhu, J. Cao, Z.-G. Gu, Z. Zhao, L. Shen, B.Z. Tang, A highly luminescent entangled metal–organic framework based on pyridine-substituted tetraphenylethene for efficient pesticide detection, Chem. Commun. 53 (2017) 9975–9978. doi: 10.1039/C7CC05031C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crosslink-enhanced emission effect on luminescence in polymers: advances and perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tao, S. Zhu, T. Feng, C. Zheng, B. Yang, Crosslink-Enhanced Emission Effect on Luminescence in Polymers: Advances and Perspectives, Angew. Chemie Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 9826–9840. doi: 10.1002/anie.201916591.

Yang, The crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) in non-conjugated polymer dots: from the photoluminescence mechanism to the cellular uptake mechanism and internalization, Chem. Commun. 50 (2014) 13845–13848. doi: 10.1039/C4CC05806B.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-conjugated polymer dots with crosslink-enhanced emission in the absence of fluorophore units

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, J. Shao, X. Zhao, B. Yang, Non-Conjugated Polymer Dots with Crosslink-Enhanced Emission in the Absence of Fluorophore Units, Angew. Chemie Int. Ed. 54 (2015) 14626–14637. doi: 10.1002/anie.201504951.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: a review on mechanisms and applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optically-active nanocrystals for inner filter effect-based fluorescence sensing: achieving better spectral overlap, TrAC
